summary: DHH announces that 37signals is moving continuous integration from remote BuildKite runners back to local developer machines. Modern multi-core CPUs like the Intel 14900K and Apple M3 Max can run their full test suite in under half the time of their cloud CI setup. He argues this represents a broader pattern of using hardware improvements to simplify development stacks rather than adding capability. He acknowledges this won't work for massive codebases like Shopify or GitHub, but contends that 99.99% of web apps are small enough to benefit from this approach.
key_points: Local developer machines now outperform remote CI runners for HEY's test suite, completing in under 2.5 minutes vs 5.5 minutes remotely
Modern CPUs with 8-20+ cores make parallelized local testing feasible in ways that were unthinkable in the mid-2010s
The real benefit isn't speed but simplification — eliminating the operational complexity of remote CI infrastructure
Hardware progress should be spent on simplifying stacks, not adding complexity: #nobuild, local CI, dropping Spring
Most web apps are small enough to run CI locally, even if tech giants with millions of lines of code cannot
money_quotes: Getting to flush all of it down the simplification drain is an amazing step forward.
Never aspire to a more complicated stack than what your application calls for.
Small teams ought to remove all the moving parts possible.
Installing, operating, and caring for a remote CI setup is a substantial complication.
The simplified future is not evenly distributed.
Running all these checks and validations in a reasonable time on a local machine would have been unthinkable not too long ago.
key_insight: When hardware gets faster, the best use of that performance gain is eliminating infrastructure complexity rather than scaling up what you build on top of it.
